title: Adana kebabı
subtitle: Spiced minced lamb on wide skewers, from the southern Turkish city
excerpt: Hand-minced lamb mixed with grated onion, parsley, and plenty of chilli, shaped into flat sausages and grilled over fierce heat. The mince must be worked until cohesive so it holds its shape. Served with flatbread, raw onion, and a dusting of sumac.
sourceNotes: Adana kebabı takes its name from Adana, the fourth-largest city in Turkey, situated in the Çukurova plain in the country's south. The preparation is documented in Turkish culinary literature from the late Ottoman period and is a protected geographical indication in Turkey. The key distinctions from other kofta preparations are the use of lamb (not beef), the high chilli content using the local biber salçası (red pepper paste), and the flat, wide skewer shape.

content.text: Adana kebab is defined by its heat. The original uses biber salçası, a Turkish red pepper paste that is simultaneously fruity, sweet, and fiercely spicy. In the absence of biber salçası, chilli flakes and a teaspoon of paprika get close. The lamb should be reasonably fatty: 20 per cent fat content is about right. Lean mince will fall apart on the grill and taste dry.

The key to kebabs that hold their shape is working the mince. Knead the mixture with your hands for 3 to 4 minutes until it becomes smooth, cohesive, and slightly sticky. This develops the myosin proteins in the meat, which act as a natural binder. If the mixture still crumbles after kneading, refrigerate it for 30 minutes before shaping.

items.name: Lamb mince
items.prepNote: at least 20% fat; ask a butcher to mince it twice if possible

items.name: Onion
items.prepNote: finely grated; squeeze out excess liquid

items.name: Chilli flakes
items.prepNote: or use 1 tbsp Turkish biber salçası if available

items.name: Red onion
items.prepNote: thinly sliced, tossed with sumac and parsley

content.text: Grate the {{onion}} onion on the fine side of a box grater. Squeeze out as much liquid as possible in a clean cloth or kitchen paper. Combine the {{lamb-mince}} with the grated onion, the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley, the {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, the {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, the {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, and the {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Knead with your hands for 3 to 4 minutes until the mixture is very smooth and slightly sticky. Cover and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes.

Divide the mixture into 8 equal portions. With wet hands, shape each portion into a flat, elongated sausage about 15 cm long and 3 cm wide. If using metal skewers, thread each kebab onto a flat skewer. If cooking in a pan or on a griddle, shape as above and chill on a plate for 10 minutes until firm.

Heat a dry griddle pan or heavy frying pan over high heat until very hot. Cook the kebabs for 4 to 5 minutes without moving until well charred on the base. Turn carefully with tongs and cook for 3 to 4 minutes on the other side. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve on or alongside the warmed flatbread, with the sliced {{red-onion}} tossed with {{sumac}} and extra parsley.

items.fix: Knead for the full 3 to 4 minutes; squeeze all liquid from the grated onion; use mince with at least 20% fat
items.cause: Mince not kneaded enough; too much liquid from the onion; or lean mince used
items.symptom: Kebabs fall apart on the grill

items.fix: Use fattier mince; cook to just past pink. They should be juicy inside when cut
items.symptom: Kebabs are dry and taste grainy

items.fix: Heat the pan until smoking before adding the kebabs; do not move them for the first 4 minutes
items.cause: Pan not hot enough; moisture on the surface of the kebab

items.fix: Taste the raw mixture. It should taste aggressively seasoned before cooking; add more chilli and salt if needed
items.cause: Not enough chilli, or chilli lost potency
items.symptom: Flavour is bland and not spicy enough

content.text: Adana is a city on the Çukurova plain in southern Turkey. The region has been a centre of farming and trade for thousands of years. The kebab that bears the city's name is different from milder Urfa kebab, which uses less chilli and more fat. It is also different from the round kofta of central Turkey. Adana kebabı is a protected name in Turkey. The Turkish rules set the fat ratio, the cut of lamb, and the grinding method. Outside Turkey, you find it in Turkish food shops across Europe. It has become the template for grilled spiced lamb dishes across the Middle East.
